extraversion.label: Presence
extraversion.description: How much energy you bring to social spaces.
extraversion.blockIntro: These questions explore how you show up in social situations and connect with others.

agreeableness.label: Bond
agreeableness.description: How you relate to others and the warmth you bring.
agreeableness.blockIntro: These questions explore how you build trust, show care, and relate to the people around you.

conscientiousness.label: Discipline
conscientiousness.description: How you approach tasks, goals, and follow-through.
conscientiousness.blockIntro: These questions explore how you organise your time, keep commitments, and stay on track.

negativeEmotionality.label: Depth
negativeEmotionality.description: How you process emotions and navigate uncertainty.
negativeEmotionality.blockIntro: These questions explore your emotional landscape — how you respond to stress, change, and inner tension.

openMindedness.label: Vision
openMindedness.description: How you engage with ideas, beauty, and new thinking.
openMindedness.blockIntro: These questions explore your curiosity, imagination, and openness to new perspectives.

depth.name: Depth
depth.description: How you process emotions and navigate uncertainty.
depth.blockIntro: These questions explore your emotional landscape — how you respond to stress, change, and inner tension.

presence.name: Presence
presence.description: How much energy you bring to social spaces.
presence.blockIntro: These questions explore how you show up in social situations and connect with others.

vision.name: Vision
vision.description: How you engage with ideas, beauty, and new thinking.
vision.blockIntro: These questions explore your curiosity, imagination, and openness to new perspectives.

bond.name: Bond
bond.description: How you relate to others and the warmth you bring.
bond.blockIntro: These questions explore how you build trust, show care, and relate to the people around you.

discipline.name: Discipline
discipline.description: How you approach tasks, goals, and follow-through.
discipline.blockIntro: These questions explore how you organise your time, keep commitments, and stay on track.

presence.high: You bring energy into a room before you've said a word. Groups gravitate toward you, not because you try, but because stillness isn't your natural state. You think out loud, and that pulls others in.
presence.low: You don't need the room to notice you. Your energy goes inward first, which means when you do speak, it lands. You observe more than most, and that's not a small thing.

bond.high: You feel what others feel, sometimes before they do. Conflict costs you more than it costs others, and you'll often smooth things over just to keep the air clear. That's a gift, as long as you don't disappear in the process.
bond.low: You're not here to be liked, you're here to be useful. You'll say the hard thing when others won't, and you don't need consensus to feel confident. Groups need someone like you, even when they don't admit it.

discipline.high: You finish what you start. When you say something will be done, it gets done. Structure isn't a constraint for you, it's how you build things that last. Others rely on that more than they say.
discipline.low: Rules feel like someone else's answer to a problem you'd solve differently. You're better at starting things than finishing them, which means you need people around you who love the last 20%. You probably already know who they are.

depth.high: You feel things fully. That means the hard moments hit harder, but it also means you pick up on things others miss entirely. Your sensitivity isn't a flaw in the system, it's how the system gets calibrated.
depth.low: Pressure doesn't rattle you. When things go sideways, you're often the one others look to, not because you're unaffected, but because you don't show it. That steadiness is rarer than it looks.

vision.high: You're drawn to the idea before the plan, the question before the answer. New things don't scare you, sameness does. You'll often see where something could go before anyone else has looked up from where they are.
vision.low: You trust what works. Not every problem needs a new solution, and you know that. When everyone else is chasing the next thing, you're making sure the current thing actually holds together. That's underrated.

sociability.label: Sociability
sociability.high: You don't do well alone for long. People are where you recharge, where you think, where you come alive. Put you in a room and you'll have talked to half of it before the event starts.
sociability.low: You're selective about who gets your time and energy. Small talk costs you something. When you do connect, it goes deep, and that tends to matter more than volume.

assertiveness.label: Assertiveness
assertiveness.high: You don't wait for permission. When something needs to be said or decided, you say it or decide it. That clarity cuts through noise, and most groups need more of it than they have.
assertiveness.low: You'd rather observe before acting. You give others space to lead, and when you do step in, it's because you've already thought it through. That deliberateness isn't hesitation, it's precision.

energyLevel.label: Energy Level
energyLevel.high: You run hot. Long meetings, back-to-back days, demanding projects, you tend to find a second gear where others are already done. The risk is you don't always notice when the people around you have stopped keeping up.
energyLevel.low: You're sustainable. You know your pace and you keep it. You won't burn bright and crash, which makes you someone others can count on when a project runs long.

compassion.label: Compassion
compassion.high: When someone in the room is struggling, you notice before they say anything. You adjust. That awareness makes people feel seen, which is one of the most useful things one person can do for another.
compassion.low: You don't take on other people's emotional weight automatically. That's not coldness, it's a different kind of clarity. You focus on the problem, not the feelings around it, and sometimes that's exactly what's needed.

respectfulness.label: Respectfulness
respectfulness.high: You treat everyone as if they have something worth hearing. That means you listen more carefully than most, disagree without dismissing, and rarely make people feel small. That builds trust faster than almost anything else.
respectfulness.low: You're direct, sometimes bluntly so. You don't adjust your tone to make people comfortable, and you don't think you should have to. That can land hard, but it also means people always know where they stand with you.

trust.label: Trust
trust.high: Your default is to believe people mean well. That makes you easy to work with and generous with second chances. The downside is you can be slow to see it when someone isn't playing straight.
trust.low: You don't take things at face value. You watch, you test, you verify. That scepticism has probably saved you more than once. It also means trust, when you give it, actually means something.

organization.label: Organization
organization.high: You know where everything is, when everything is due, and what happens if it's not. That mental order frees you to focus, and it makes working with you feel reliable in a way that's hard to replace.
organization.low: Your workspace might look like chaos, but you know where things are, mostly. You work in bursts and manage by priority, not by system. What you lose in tidiness you sometimes gain in speed.

productiveness.label: Productiveness
productiveness.high: You're here to get things done. Not to talk about getting things done, not to plan getting things done. Done. That output orientation is rare and it drives results in ways that are hard to argue with.
productiveness.low: You're not motivated by output for its own sake. You need a reason, a purpose, something that matters. When you have it, you work hard. When you don't, you won't pretend.

responsibility.label: Responsibility
responsibility.high: If you said you'd do it, it's done. Full stop. People stop worrying about things they've handed to you, and that's a form of trust that takes a long time to build and matters enormously in a team.
responsibility.low: You're better at big commitments than small ones. Rules, deadlines and obligations that feel arbitrary tend to slide. You'll show up for what genuinely matters, but you need to believe it matters first.

anxiety.label: Anxiety
anxiety.high: You anticipate what could go wrong, often before anyone else has thought to look. That vigilance catches real problems. The cost is that the alarm is sometimes running even when everything is fine.
anxiety.low: You don't lose sleep over what might happen. You deal with what does happen, when it happens. That calm under uncertainty is something teams need more of, especially when things get complicated.

depression.label: Mood
depression.high: You carry things. Setbacks don't just bounce off you, they settle in for a while. That depth of feeling means you take things seriously in a way that shallow people never will. It's worth something, even when it doesn't feel like it.
depression.low: You recover fast. Bad days don't trail into bad weeks. That resilience isn't the absence of feeling, it's the ability to move through things without getting stuck. Groups need that kind of ballast.

emotionalVolatility.label: Emotional Steadiness
emotionalVolatility.high: Your reactions are visible. When something's wrong, people know. When something's right, they know that too. That transparency can be unsettling, but it also means you're never performing a mood you don't feel.
emotionalVolatility.low: You keep an even keel regardless of what's happening around you. People can't easily read you, which can frustrate them, but it also means you don't add noise when the situation already has enough.

intellectualCuriosity.label: Intellectual Curiosity
intellectualCuriosity.high: You want to understand how things work, not just that they work. Give you a problem and you'll pull it apart before you try to fix it. That depth of inquiry finds things that surface-level thinking misses every time.
intellectualCuriosity.low: You're a practitioner, not a theorist. You want tools that work, explanations that help, answers you can use. Not every question needs to be opened back up, and you know which ones don't.

aestheticSensitivity.label: Aesthetic Sensitivity
aestheticSensitivity.high: You notice when something is beautiful and when it isn't. That attention to form isn't superficial, it's how you process quality. You'll often feel that something is wrong before you can say why, and you're usually right.
aestheticSensitivity.low: Function matters more to you than form. If it works, it works. You're not going to spend time making something beautiful when making it better is still on the table. That focus on substance keeps things moving.

creativeImagination.label: Creative Imagination
creativeImagination.high: You think in possibilities. The current situation is always just one version of what could be. That ability to imagine alternatives is where new ideas come from, and new ideas are what teams need when the old answers stop working.
creativeImagination.low: You're grounded in what exists, not what might. You'd rather improve what's here than invent something new. That realism stops a lot of bad ideas from going too far, which is a more valuable role than people think.

vigil.label: Vigil
vigil.high: You run threat detection constantly. Most of the time there is nothing there, but sometimes you catch something real before anyone else has looked up. That vigilance is exhausting and occasionally invaluable.
vigil.low: You don't borrow trouble from the future. You deal with what is in front of you, when it arrives. That groundedness is rarer than it looks, especially when things get uncertain.

blaze.label: Blaze
blaze.high: Your frustration surfaces quickly and visibly. People always know where they stand with you, which is not nothing. The cost is that small things sometimes get the same reaction as big things.
blaze.low: It takes a lot to rattle you into anger. You don't escalate, you don't match other people's heat. In tense moments, that steadiness changes the temperature of the whole room.

hollow.label: Hollow
hollow.high: You carry weight. Setbacks settle in rather than bounce off. That depth of feeling means you take things seriously in a way that lighter people never will. It costs you, and it's worth something.
hollow.low: You recover fast. Bad days don't become bad weeks. That capacity to move forward isn't indifference, it's a kind of resilience that keeps you functional when others are still processing.

veil.label: Veil
veil.high: You're aware of how you come across, sometimes acutely so. That social sensitivity makes you careful and considerate. It also means you spend energy on how things land that others never think about.
veil.low: You don't waste much energy worrying about what people think of you. That ease with yourself comes across as confidence, even when it's just comfort. Either way, it tends to put others at ease too.

surge.label: Surge
surge.high: You act on what you feel, often before the thought is fully formed. Sometimes that speed is the whole advantage. Sometimes it isn't, and you know that too.
surge.low: You don't get swept up easily. Urges pass, reactions get filtered, decisions get considered. That self-regulation is the quiet engine behind a lot of things that actually go well.

fracture.label: Fracture
fracture.high: When pressure builds, you feel it fully. That sensitivity isn't weakness, it's a signal. You pick up on strain before others do, which means you're often the one who says something needs to change.
fracture.low: You hold up under pressure without visible cracks. People look to you when things get hard, not because you're unaffected, but because you keep functioning. That's load-bearing work in any team.

hearth.label: Hearth
hearth.high: People feel welcome around you without knowing exactly why. You make the effort to connect, to remember, to ask. That warmth is not small talk, it's the foundation of trust.
hearth.low: You don't warm up instantly and you don't pretend to. The connections you do make are genuine and durable. People who know you well know they're getting something real.

gather.label: Gather
gather.high: Groups energize you. The more people, the better you tend to function. You're the one who shows up early and leaves late, and the room usually notices when you're not there.
gather.low: You can work a room when you need to, but it costs you something. You're better in smaller configurations, where depth is possible. Quality over volume, every time.

command.label: Command
command.high: You don't wait for permission. When something needs deciding, you decide it. When something needs saying, you say it. That directness cuts through noise and most groups need more of it.
command.low: You observe before you act. You let others lead, and when you do step in it's because you've already thought it through. That's not hesitation, it's precision.

drive.label: Drive
drive.high: You run at a high tempo. Back-to-back days, multiple projects, long meetings, you find a second gear where others are already done. Just check that the people around you are still keeping up.
drive.low: You're sustainable. You know your pace and you keep it. You won't burn bright and crash, which makes you someone others can count on when a project runs longer than expected.

thrill.label: Thrill
thrill.high: Routine is where your attention goes to die. You need stakes, novelty, something on the line. That drive toward intensity is where a lot of your best work comes from.
thrill.low: You don't need adrenaline to function well. Calm, steady environments bring out your best. You're the one who keeps working when everyone else is distracted by the drama.

radiance.label: Radiance
radiance.high: Your enthusiasm is contagious and genuine. You bring energy to things before they've earned it, which sometimes makes them earn it. Groups feel different when you're in them.
radiance.low: You don't perform enthusiasm you don't feel. Your approval means something because you don't give it automatically. When you say something is good, people believe you.

dream.label: Dream
dream.high: Your mind goes places unprompted. Daydreaming isn't distraction for you, it's where ideas come from. The internal world is rich, and it feeds the external one in ways that aren't always visible.
dream.low: You stay close to the ground. What's real, what's here, what works. That focus keeps you useful in ways that dreamers sometimes aren't.

craft.label: Craft
craft.high: You notice when something is beautiful and when it isn't. That attention to form isn't superficial, it's how you process quality. You feel something is wrong before you can say why, and you're usually right.
craft.low: Function matters more to you than form. If it works, it works. You won't spend time making something beautiful when making it better is still on the table.

resonance.label: Resonance
resonance.high: You're tuned into emotional register, yours and other people's. That attunement makes you a careful reader of rooms and a thoughtful communicator. It also means you carry more than most.
resonance.low: You lead with logic rather than feeling. That keeps things clear and uncluttered. In emotionally charged situations, your levelheadedness is often exactly what's needed.

drift.label: Drift
drift.high: Variety keeps you engaged. New approaches, different contexts, unfamiliar territory, that's where you do your best thinking. Repetition is where you lose interest fastest.
drift.low: You trust what works and you stick with it. Not every problem needs a new solution. When others are chasing novelty, you're making sure the current thing actually holds together.

prism.label: Prism
prism.high: You want to understand how things work, not just that they work. Give you a problem and you'll pull it apart before you try to fix it. That depth of inquiry finds things surface-level thinking misses.
prism.low: You're a practitioner, not a theorist. You want tools that work, explanations you can use. Not every question needs to be opened back up, and you know which ones don't.

compass.label: Compass
compass.high: You question inherited rules and established norms. Not out of rebellion, but because you think things through and don't assume the default is right. That willingness to reconsider is how things improve.
compass.low: You value stability, proven systems, and shared norms. Not everything needs to be questioned, and you know which things don't. That conservatism keeps a lot of things from breaking unnecessarily.

faith.label: Faith
faith.high: Your default is to believe people mean well. That generosity makes you easy to work with and quick to give second chances. The risk is you're sometimes slow to see it when someone isn't playing straight.
faith.low: You don't take things at face value. You watch, you test, you verify. That scepticism has probably saved you more than once. And when you do trust someone, it actually means something.

edge.label: Edge
edge.high: What you say is what you mean. No performance, no managing impressions, no strategic ambiguity. People always know where they stand with you, which is more valuable than it sounds.
edge.low: You're skilled at navigating social complexity. You know when to hold back, when to frame things carefully, when directness would cost more than it's worth. That's not dishonesty, it's sophistication.

gift.label: Gift
gift.high: You make people feel welcome without effort. You notice when someone is on the outside and you do something about it. That generosity of attention is one of the most underrated things a person can bring to a group.
gift.low: You're not here to take care of everyone. You focus your energy where it matters and don't spread it thin on obligation. That focus makes what you do give actually count.

yield.label: Yield
yield.high: You'd rather find agreement than win the argument. That collaborative instinct keeps things moving and keeps relationships intact. Just make sure you're choosing peace rather than defaulting to it.
yield.low: You push back. You don't capitulate to social pressure and you don't pretend to agree when you don't. Groups need that friction, even when they don't enjoy it in the moment.

shadow.label: Shadow
shadow.high: You don't take up more space than you need. Credit gets shared, achievements don't get announced. That restraint builds a kind of trust that self-promotion never does.
shadow.low: You know what you're worth and you don't hide it. That confidence in your own value isn't arrogance, it's clarity. And sometimes the room needs someone who's willing to say what they bring.

shield.label: Shield
shield.high: You feel the impact of things on people who aren't in the room. That ethical attentiveness means you think about consequences others miss entirely. It slows things down sometimes, for good reason.
shield.low: You focus on facts and results rather than feelings. That objectivity keeps analysis clean and decisions grounded. Not every situation needs an emotional audit before moving forward.

mastery.label: Mastery
mastery.high: You handle things well and you know it. That confidence in your own capability isn't arrogance, it's accurate. You don't second-guess yourself into paralysis because you've usually done the work.
mastery.low: You're still figuring things out, and you're honest about it. That openness to learning keeps you from the overconfidence that trips up people who think they've already arrived.

structure.label: Structure
structure.high: You know where everything is, when everything is due, and what happens if it's not. That mental order frees you to focus, and it makes working with you feel reliable in a way that's hard to replace.
structure.low: Your workspace might look like chaos, but you know where things are, mostly. You manage by priority, not by system. What you lose in tidiness you sometimes gain in speed.

oath.label: Oath
oath.high: If you said you'd do it, it's done. People stop worrying about things they've handed to you, and that's a form of trust that takes a long time to build and matters enormously in a team.
oath.low: You're better at big commitments than small ones. Obligations that feel arbitrary tend to slide. You'll show up for what genuinely matters, but you need to believe it matters first.

quest.label: Quest
quest.high: You're here to get things done. Not to talk about it, not to plan it. Done. That output orientation is rare and it drives results in ways that are hard to argue with.
quest.low: You're not motivated by output for its own sake. You need a reason, a purpose, something that matters. When you have it, you work hard. When you don't, you won't pretend.

will.label: Will
will.high: You start things when they need starting, not when you feel like it. That ability to act without waiting for motivation is how things that matter actually get finished.
will.low: You need the right conditions to get going. Pressure, interest, meaning. Without them, starting is hard. With them, you can go deep in ways that people who just grind never reach.

counsel.label: Counsel
counsel.high: You think before you act. Decisions get considered, words get chosen, consequences get mapped. That care prevents a lot of problems that impulsive people have to clean up later.
counsel.low: You decide fast and adjust as you go. That speed has a cost sometimes, but it also means you're moving while others are still deliberating. In fast-moving situations, that matters.

R01.name: Dolphin
R01.essence: When you are in the room, people talk. Not because you ask — because you made it easy. The team warms up with you.
R01.profile: High Presence and Bond. Warm, outgoing, and easy to be around. People open up in your company.
R01.contributes: Cohesion. Lowers the social activation energy in the room.
R01.misses: The productive friction that sharpens ideas. Warmth can smooth over real disagreements.

R02.name: Wolf
R02.essence: You do not wait for permission. When you see the problem, you say it. The team moves because you do not stay quiet.
R02.profile: High Presence, low Bond. Direct, assertive, and action-oriented. Does not wait for consensus.
R02.contributes: Initiative. Names what needs to be said and gets things moving.
R02.misses: The relational cost of directness over time. Teams can lose trust if the challenge is constant.

R03.name: Elephant
R03.essence: You do not take up space — you create it. People explain themselves well beside you because they know you are really listening.
R03.profile: Low Presence, high Bond. Quiet and deeply cooperative. Creates space for others to think out loud.
R03.contributes: Depth of listening. People articulate themselves better in your presence.
R03.misses: Momentum. The team can think clearly together but still not move.

R04.name: Owl
R04.essence: You see what others miss because you do not need to be at the centre. The distance is useful to you.
R04.profile: Low Presence and Bond. Independent, observant, and precise. Works best with distance.
R04.contributes: Clarity. Sees structural problems that high-social profiles miss.
R04.misses: Warmth and easy collaboration. The distance that helps analysis can isolate.

R05.name: Eagle
R05.essence: You see far and you move there. You bring the team to places it would not have gone alone.
R05.profile: High Presence and Vision. Energetic and future-oriented. Pulls the team toward new ground.
R05.contributes: Direction. Sets ambitious goals the team would not have set alone.
R05.misses: Execution detail. The horizon is clear; the path can be rough.

R06.name: Falcon
R06.essence: It is not vision — it is timing. You know exactly when to move, and when you do, the team notices.
R06.profile: High Presence, low Vision. Present, decisive, and practical. Knows exactly when to act.
R06.contributes: Timing. Moves at the right moment and carries people with them.
R06.misses: Long-horizon thinking. Can miss the strategic layer while executing well at speed.

R07.name: Octopus
R07.essence: The team's best ideas often passed through you first, in silence. You do not always know it yourself.
R07.profile: Low Presence, high Vision. Internally rich and quietly creative. Processes complexity in private.
R07.contributes: Original thinking. The team's most unusual ideas often come from here.
R07.misses: Getting ideas into circulation. Works in silence; the ideas can stay there.

R08.name: Tortoise
R08.essence: You will not make noise. But when you are not there, the team drifts. You are the ground the others build on.
R08.profile: Low Presence and Vision. Steady, grounded, and deeply reliable. Holds the ground so others can move.
R08.contributes: Stability. Others can take risks because this profile holds the ground.
R08.misses: Adaptability when the situation fundamentally changes.

R09.name: Bee
R09.essence: Where others see chaos, you see structure. You build bridges between people and ideas no one else would have connected.
R09.profile: High Bond and Vision. Collaborative and intellectually generative. Connects people and ideas.
R09.contributes: Synthesis. Builds bridges between people and between concepts no one else had linked.
R09.misses: The critical edge needed to test whether the connections hold.

R10.name: Bear
R10.essence: You do not change under pressure. The team knows you are there, and that knowing frees them to take risks.
R10.profile: High Bond, low Vision. Dependable, grounded, and consistent. The team knows where it stands.
R10.contributes: Trust. People take more risks when a Bear profile is present.
R10.misses: Openness to change when the current approach is no longer working.

R11.name: Fox
R11.essence: You see what does not add up — in ideas and in relationships. Your discomfort is productive.
R11.profile: Low Bond, high Vision. Intellectually sharp and critically minded. Spots inconsistency fast.
R11.contributes: Critical distance. Catches what does not add up before it becomes a problem.
R11.misses: Relational ease. The discomfort can wear on a team that needs cohesion to function.

R12.name: Badger
R12.essence: You are not interested in what should work. You are interested in what works. The team needs you to stop fooling itself.
R12.profile: Low Bond and Vision. Pragmatic, direct, and unsentimental. What works matters; what should work does not.
R12.contributes: Ground truth. Stops the team fooling itself with optimism or complexity.
R12.misses: Buy-in. The directness that surfaces truth can also create resistance.

setup.title: Invite your witnesses
setup.subtitle: A witness sees you from the outside. Add up to 12 people who know you well.
setup.shareInstruction: Send each link to the person named. Each link works once.

intro.heading: You've been asked to describe someone.
intro.body: You'll see groups of words and pick which one fits this person best — and which fits least. Takes about 5 minutes.

page.instructionSub: Pick one for Best fit and one for Worst fit.
page.selectBothNote: Pick one Best and one Worst to continue.

done.heading: Done.
done.body: Your observations have been recorded. Thank you.

alreadyDone.heading: Already submitted.
alreadyDone.body: This link has already been used. Each link can only be submitted once.

notFound.heading: Link not found.
notFound.body: Check the link and try again.

page.errorBody: We couldn't save your response. Try again later.

witnessResults.title: Full Moon report
witnessResults.subtitle: Self-report · Witness Cèrcol
witnessResults.error: Could not load report. Try again later.
witnessResults.noSelf: Complete Full Moon Cèrcol first to see your report.
witnessResults.pendingNote: You need at least 2 completed witnesses to see this section.
witnessResults.divergenceNote: Dimensions where your self-report and witness reports differ significantly.

divergenceDirection.higher: Witnesses rate {{domain}} higher than you do.
divergenceDirection.lower: Witnesses rate {{domain}} lower than you do.

witnessResults.noDivergence: Strong alignment. Your self-perception closely matches how others see you.
witnessResults.disclaimer: Witness results reflect the perspective of the people who completed the instrument. Divergence is not a flaw — it is information.

intro.heading: See yourself more clearly.
intro.body: Cèrcol is three instruments built on public-domain science. Each measures the same five dimensions — at different depths. No score tells you what to think. No result is good or bad.
intro.founding: Tot suma, ningú no és imprescindible, però tots som necessaris.

philosophy.heading: Built in the open
philosophy.body: Most personality assessments are proprietary. The items, the scoring, the normative data — behind paywalls or NDAs. You take the test, get a result, and have no way to know if the algorithm is sound. Cèrcol is different. Every question comes from public-domain research. The scoring is on GitHub. Anyone can read it, question it, and improve it. We think personality assessments should work that way.

instruments.label: Instruments
instruments.desc: How each instrument works, what it measures, and what you get.

roles.label: Roles
roles.desc: The nine roles, where they come from, and their honest limitations.

science.label: Science
science.desc: The research foundation, the scoring pipeline, and the open questions.

dimensions.heading: Five dimensions
dimensions.subheading: Every score sits on a spectrum. Both ends are valid — they describe different ways of being effective.

presence.name: Presence
presence.desc: How much energy you direct outward. High: you bring people in. Low: you go deep with fewer.

bond.name: Bond
bond.desc: How you connect and cooperate. High: you prioritise trust and harmony. Low: you hold your ground.

discipline.name: Discipline
discipline.desc: How you approach structure and work. High: reliable, methodical. Low: flexible, instinct-driven.

depth.name: Depth
depth.desc: How intensely you process stress and emotion. High: sensitive, alert. Low: stable, hard to rattle.

vision.name: Vision
vision.desc: How much novelty and complexity you seek. High: creative, exploratory. Low: practical, grounded.

notThis.heading: What Cèrcol is not
notThis.item1: A diagnostic tool. These results have no clinical validity.
notThis.item2: A fixed label. Scores shift with context, time, and state of mind.
notThis.item3: A ranking. No dimension is better or worse than another.
notThis.item4: A predictor of success. Tendencies describe how you tend to operate — not what you'll achieve.

framing.heading: A note on scores
framing.body: These are tendencies, not labels. The same trait that creates friction in one context is exactly what's needed in another. Low Discipline isn't failure — it's often what keeps things moving when the plan falls apart.

openInstruments.heading: No proprietary tests.
openInstruments.body: Every question in Cèrcol comes from a public-domain collection of personality items used in hundreds of peer-reviewed studies. No licensing restrictions, no black boxes. Anyone can read the items, run the scoring, and verify the results.

fiveFactors.heading: The most replicated finding in personality psychology.
fiveFactors.body: Across cultures, languages, and methods, research consistently recovers the same five broad dimensions of personality. Cèrcol uses these under different names — Presence, Bond, Discipline, Depth, Vision — to make the language warmer without changing what's being measured.
fiveFactors.note: Replicated in more than 50 countries. The strongest consensus result in differential psychology.

roles.heading: Twelve roles. Derived from the research, not invented.
roles.body: The role taxonomy is grounded in the AB5C circumplex (Hofstee, De Raad & Goldberg, 1992) and the team composition literature (Bell, 2007). Three OCEAN dimensions — Presence, Bond, and Vision — require balance in teams. The twelve roles cover the six intersections of these three dimensions at both poles. Discipline and Depth modulate how each role is expressed.
roles.beta: Current role assignments use theoretical centroids derived from published research. As real data accumulates — target: 300 Full Moon completions — centroids will be replaced with empirical values from actual profiles.
roles.betaNote: Role scoring is labelled beta in the interface. It is illustrative, not diagnostic.

fullMoon.heading: Two lenses on the same person.

selfReport.heading: Self-report
selfReport.body: 120 items across 5 domains and 30 facets — twice the resolution of First Quarter. Forms the primary role result.

witness.heading: Witness Cèrcol
witness.body: A forced-choice task given to someone who knows you well. Each round, the Witness sees five words and picks the best fit and worst fit for you — one adjective per dimension. Forced choice eliminates the social desirability bias that distorts self-report.
witness.idealWitness: An ideal Witness has worked with you, lived with you, or spent real time around you. Not your closest ally, not your harshest critic — someone who sees you as you actually are.
witness.blindSpots: The Witness result is compared to the self-report. Where they diverge significantly, a blind spot is flagged — a dimension you see differently than the people around you do.

openSource.heading: The code is open.
openSource.body: Cèrcol is open source. The scoring logic, item corpus, and role algorithm are all readable.

q1.a: The free instruments log anonymous scores with no personal information — no name, no email, no IP address. If you create an account, your email is stored by Supabase and your results are linked to your account. You can delete your account at any time. No data is sold or shared with third parties.

q2.a: The questions come from peer-reviewed, widely-validated instruments. The five-dimension scores are reliable for a self-report tool of this length. The role scoring is clearly labelled beta — it uses theoretical starting points and will become more accurate as real data accumulates. Take the dimensions seriously; treat the role as a useful first guess.

q3.q: What is the Witness and who should I invite?
q3.a: The Witness is someone you invite to describe you from the outside. They see groups of words and pick which ones fit you best and worst — a short forced-choice task that takes about 5 minutes. An ideal Witness is someone who has spent real time with you: a colleague, a housemate, a close friend. Not your biggest fan, not your harshest critic. Someone who sees you as you actually are.

q4.q: What is the difference between the three instruments?
q4.a: New Moon is 10 questions and takes 2 minutes — a quick orientation. First Quarter is 60 questions and takes 10 minutes — a fuller picture with 30 facets and a first look at your role. Full Moon is 120 questions, includes the Witness assessment, and is paid — it is the definitive result. Each instrument is standalone; you can start anywhere.

q5.q: Is Full Moon worth paying for?
q5.a: If First Quarter feels insufficient — if you want to understand a specific facet more deeply, see how others perceive you, or get a reliable role result — then yes. The Witness comparison is the most distinctive part: it shows where your self-perception matches the outside view and where it doesn't. That gap is often where the most useful information lives.

q6.a: Yes. The free instruments have no limit. Each submission is independent — retaking does not overwrite previous results in your account. People often find it useful to retake First Quarter six months or a year later to see what has changed.

intro.heading: Three instruments. One scale.
intro.body: Each instrument measures the same five dimensions — at different depth and resolution. They are designed to be used in sequence, but each stands alone.

newMoon.heading: New Moon Cèrcol
newMoon.scaleNote: 7-point scale: 'Disagree strongly' to 'Agree strongly'. Each point carries meaning — not just two extremes with numbers in between.
newMoon.measures: Five dimensions. One score per dimension. No facet breakdown.
newMoon.get: A radar chart of your position on all five dimensions. Good for a quick orientation. Not precise enough for fine-grained claims about facets — that's what First Quarter is for.

firstQuarter.heading: First Quarter Cèrcol
firstQuarter.scaleNote: 5-point scale: 'Disagree strongly' to 'Agree strongly'. Five-point scales are standard for IPIP facet-level measurement.
firstQuarter.measures: Five dimensions, each broken into 6 facets — 30 data points. Two items per facet.
firstQuarter.get: Domain chart, 30-facet breakdown, and a first beta read on your role. The facet breakdown is where the useful information lives — the domain totals are just the headline.
firstQuarter.itemPrefix: I see myself as someone who:

fullMoon.heading: Full Moon Cèrcol
fullMoon.system: Full Moon is a system of two parts used together.

part1.label: Part 1
part1.heading: Self-report
part1.body: The same 30 facets as First Quarter — at twice the resolution. Four items per facet instead of two. In psychometric terms, more items per facet means more reliable scores, especially for facets near the centre of the scale where two items can give a noisy result.
part1.itemPrefix: I see myself as someone who:

part2.label: Part 2
part2.heading: Witness Cèrcol
part2.whyBody: Self-reports have a known limitation: we don't always perceive ourselves the way we come across to others. People high on Depth may under-report — the emotion feels normal from the inside. People high on Bond may over-report warmth — the intent is genuine but the impact can differ. Self-assessment is a filtered signal. The Witness adds an independent one.
part2.howBody: Each Witness receives a unique link. The task takes about 5 minutes. They see 20 rounds of 5 words — one adjective per dimension — and pick the one that fits best and the one that fits least. Forced choice: no Likert scale, no ability to give all positive answers.
part2.whoBody: Someone who has worked with you, lived with you, or seen you under pressure. Not your biggest advocate — someone honest enough to mark where you have edges. You can invite up to 12 Witnesses. More Witnesses reduce noise: the scores are averaged.
part2.privacyBody: Witnesses never see your scores. You see the averaged result from all Witnesses — not individual responses.
part2.blindSpotsBody: Where your self-report and Witness scores diverge significantly, a blind spot is flagged. Not because one is right and the other wrong — but because the gap itself is informative. A dimension you perceive very differently than those around you is worth examining.

intro.heading: Twelve roles. One taxonomy.
intro.body1: Personality profiles describe how you are. Roles describe how you tend to move the balance of a team. The gap between those two things is where Cèrcol tries to work.
intro.body2: The twelve roles are derived from the AB5C circumplex (Hofstee, De Raad & Goldberg, 1992) and the team composition literature (Bell, 2007). Three OCEAN dimensions require balance in teams — Presence, Bond, and Vision. The twelve roles cover the six intersections of these dimensions at both poles. The names are ours. The structure is from the literature.

beta.heading: This is a starting hypothesis, not a validated instrument.
beta.body: Current role assignments use theoretical centroids derived from published research. As real data accumulates — target: 300 Full Moon completions — centroids will be replaced with empirical values from actual profiles. Role scoring is labelled beta in the interface for exactly this reason.

notBelbin.heading: Why not Belbin?
notBelbin.body: Belbin roles are widely used but have weaknesses: they are proprietary, require a trained assessor, and the psychometric foundations are contested in the literature. Cèrcol roles are derived from open, validated personality science. They are not the same thing and make no claim to be. The purpose — understanding how someone functions in a team — is similar. The approach is different.

critique.heading: See a problem with this model?
critique.body: This taxonomy is a hypothesis. If you have background in psychometrics, organisational psychology, or team dynamics and see a flaw — in the centroids, the naming, the forced-choice design, anything — open an issue.

centreNote.heading: What about profiles near the centre?
centreNote.body: There is no centre role. A profile with low scores on Presence, Bond, and Vision is better described by its Discipline and Depth values than assigned a role label with no functional content. The role result will show the closest centroid by distance, with a low maximum probability indicating a diffuse profile.

arcNote.heading: Personal arc
arcNote.body: Most role results include an arc — secondary roles that scored close to the primary in probability space. A Wolf with a Falcon arc brings initiative and decisive timing. A Bee with an Elephant arc brings synthesis and deep listening. The arc gives the result texture that a single label can't.

contextNote.heading: Roles are not destinies
contextNote.body: The same person can function differently in different team compositions. A Wolf in a team that already confronts everything may pull back. A Tortoise in a team that needs movement may accelerate. These are tendencies in context — not fixed identities. Don't use a role result as a ceiling.
